sentences.text: Yes, about two days later that we arrived to this Huta Zgoda work camp, some transport of Hungarian, all their people, I mean, all that I'm calling 35 or 40 years old, people came.
And they told us that next day that we left, the 1,200 children were taken to the crematorium.
And we always used the word only crematorium because we never knew that there is also gassing.
But this what it mean to us, the crematorium.
We still didn't believe it.
I personally did not believe it.
Well, I just-- I just was standing there like a good soldier and look at him.
And when he was passing by, just looked at him and-- I looked at him in his eyes like, you bastard.
You have to take me.
I-- I think so.
Yes.
We are talking the end of April in 1945?
We are in Gunskirchen.
It's just just probably 40 kilometers from Linz, some 10 kilometer from a city of Wels.
This is the camp where I was liberated.
The American army.
They actually did not walk into the camp.
Can I tell a short story?
In this-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so I think we arrived in Gunskirchen May 1.
And for five days, this was designated that camp, we were not given any food.
We were just drinking water from those channels that after the rain, and eating the peels of the wood.
And that was it every day.
My friends dead, and we were pulling them out from the-- from the barracks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were there five days.
Most of them that we arrived from Mauthausen, most of them died in these five days without food and anything else.
We-- I don't know if we were 3,000 or 4,000 from the 17,000 that survived.
On May 5--
That's exactly it.
Just the-- the condition, that no food.
And so most of them died from hunger.
And we were weak.
For five days, only water and-- and some green stuff from the trees.
And I think we were given only once food that from somehow, the American army brought in, sending to the camp some packages from the army-- from the American army.
They made some trade with the German army.
And we were standing in lines, and they gave us boxes.
And in this box, they throw all kind of American food, I remember, in cans.
This was the only food that we received in five days.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On May 5, Friday afternoon, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I heard the Germans were yelling, [GERMAN SPEECH] the-- the German soldiers from the watchtowers should leave.
And just across the door from our barrack was a watchtower.
And I saw the German soldier running down the stairs, the wooden stairs.
And I saw him to throw away his shotgun.
I ran out right away without anything to tell my friends.
And my brain was working towards the SS kitchen because I knew always-- [CLEARS THROAT] excuse me-- that there must be some food.
When arrive-- when I arrived there, there was already a lot of people there, but I was able to get in.
I was unable to get out from the kitchen.
So somehow, I climbed up over the heads of people, and I jumped out through the window.
I got two pieces of bread, the German army bread, hidden in my shirt.
And I ran back.
I took one bread, and I put it under a tree because the bread was very heavy, and I was full of mud from running, and it was a lot of water in the camp.
I mean, wet everywhere.
And I took one piece of bread-- I mean, one loaf of bread-- in my shirt.
And I ran into the barracks.
And my friends, my good friends, saw that I have something hidden.
They beat me up, and they steal the bread, friends that I was with them for a year together.
But I took my best friends, Robert Wassaberger and Gabi, and I told them that I have another piece of bread hidden under a tree.
I felt I was stronger than some of them.
But it was already dark.
I wasn't even looking for the bread anymore.
I told them, we leave the camp.
So actually, the American army did not even arrive to the camp.
They just-- the Germans abandoned.
We still heard fire.
We still heard shooting a few kilometers away.
But I was-- I have very good orientation.
I know exactly where I am.
And I took two-- my-- both friends.
We threw away our boots because it was May.
It wasn't too cold.
And we started to walk towards a city that we just came five days before.
I thought, in a city, we are free, so we will be OK.
We were walking probably three or four hours when on the road, we-- I-- we-- we saw a machine gun with the-- the lights are showing the machine gun, so we stopped.
Those were-- three American soldiers were approaching the camp.
They were going towards the camp.
They told-- they were terrible scared from us.
We looked, even in the dark, with those flashlights, we looked like little animals.
Not dangerous, but animals.
So they gave us chewing gum.
We didn't even know what it means, chewing gum.
From the-- from the beginning, we were chewing it because it was sweet.
Then it wasn't sweet, we just swallowed them.
But he told us-- I mean, and we showed them our numbers even without they know exactly who we are because of the clothing.
And they told us in two, three hours, we're going to reach a city, Wels, and the Red Cross is waiting for us there.
So we were walking, very hungry and very tired.
Early in the morning, we arrived to this city.
And the American army gave us food.
And they told us to go up on the roof, and we can sleep over there.
And somebody is going to take care of us.
So this was the liberation.
Yeah, it was a wonderful feeling.
We were running all over in the city to steal food.
We went into abandoned store.
We grab some dried fruit.
We put it in big bags.
And we were walking further.
Then we couldn't anymore because we found something else.
So we throw out this bag, we grabbed other bags.
And this was our-- we-- we went into a German army camp, and we established our-- our-- rooms over there.
We were bosses of this huge camp.
There were other survivors there also.
But we stayed together, three of us.
And that night, we went out to steal from the American army jeeps because we found out that the American army trucks and the jeeps, they have a lot of goodies.
So we were just stealing food.
We didn't need it so much.
But we were stealing it.
After four or five days, I got sick.
I didn't know what.
I got very, very sick with lot of fever.
And while I was walking-- waling on the street, my two friends helping me walking, an American army six-wheeler stopped with two black soldiers.
They grabbed me, and they took me on the truck, and they took me to an army hospital.
And I-- then I found out that I had typhus of my stomach.
I was very, very sick for a long time.
An army-- a Hungarian-- not a Hungarian.
An American Jewish doctor treated me.
But he knew very little Hungarian.
He didn't spoke no German.
Just because his parents probably was Hungarian.
And I was told by my friends that he healed me with hypnosis.
I don't remember too much because he told me that there is no medicine for me anymore.
After three weeks, either the typhus is cured, or it leaves the body, the-- the temperature, or the patient dies.
I just remember that he was young.
I wish I would know his name.
I never knew his name, nothing.
He was sitting many, many hours-- seemed to me hours, but maybe it was just a few minutes-- on my bedside.
And he told me that I remember that you want to be strong.
You want to come home.
Things like this.
But mostly I was in trance.
I don't remember many things.
I was very, very sick.
And about seven days later, I felt better.
In the morning, I woke up.
And I didn't have so much temperature.
And the first time, after four weeks, I was able to walk into the restroom by myself.
But of course, I was very weak and probably very ugly, just bones and skin.
And--
Yes.
After I got in the hospital, I stayed for a few days, and I felt that I was able to walk.
I went out to the city.
Actually, I ran away, because the Americans took all the Polish children to the airport, and they took them to-- to United States.
But I refused because I thought that my parents will be at home.
The Polish children, that they four or five years in the camps, they knew exactly where their parents are.
But I didn't.
I wanted to go home.

So the chocolate they used to give me every day in the hospital, and I didn't eat, I went and I bought from some Germans a pants, a shirt, and I dressed up a little bit with some shoes, and I-- and I ran away from the hospital.
You're saying the-- when they put those other children on the plane to the United States, they knew that they didn't have parents anymore, right?
Yes, the Polish children, they know.
Yeah, because in the camp we were talking.
They used to tell us that my father and mother died here, and my sister died there.
So they were alone.
They knew, most of them.
I-- we took a train.
And it took many days to get to back to Levice because the bridges were bombed out.
So we have to transfer and sleep in some-- took some-- take some shelter overnight and then grab the train next day.
And took few days.
And on the road home, I found a man from Levice who got also typhus, Fleck typhus, typhus of spots, very dangerous.
But he survived.
And he met me.
Somewhere on the road, we met.
And we too-- he was much older than I am.
I was 14 then, and he was maybe 24.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Robert Wilchuck.
I remember.
We came home to Levice.
And when the train stopped in Levice, we walked two together into the city.
And I don't know for what reason, we were walking on the middle of the road.
But there were not too many cars after the war in Levice, just closed carriages, very few cars.
We were walking middle of the street.
I still don't know what was the reason, why we didn't walk on the sidewalk.
But we looked terrible.
And everybody was looking at us.
All the people.
I didn't recognize nobody.
I was just walking.
But I didn't recognize no one.
And probably, I didn't want even to recognize.
I just was walking on the street.
And we were asking, where are the Jews, from some people that they came back from the war, and they told us where to go.
And we went into this certain building that there were already-- other survivors that came home?
It was a big Jewish building.
They came.
They received us.
And I-- I met my aunt there, that she survived the ghetto in Budapest.
And she.
Poor woman, she took care of me.
And they fed us.
The-- the-- [INAUDIBLE] gave us the food.
Her name was Helen.
Popik.
Her daughter was Magda, that I met in the concentration camp, but she divorced very young, and she took back her-- her name.
But--
Well, then, the first day.
When I came, the people that they were in Dachau with my father and with my brother, they told me how my father died, and they told me that my brother died, the survivors.
My-- my father was beaten to death on the entrance to the camp because he had some cement paper bag on his body.
They were working in a cement factory.
Middle of April, middle of April, he came, and the German guard saw that he's a little bit too heavy.
He opened and he saw paper.
And with a shotgun, he didn't shoot.
He just beat him to death next to my brother.
My brother-- my brother was liberated.
He died from Fleck typhus two, three weeks later.
Yeah.
A lot of people died.
A lot of people died.
And the mother and my little brother, I found out at home that the young mothers with the young children, they were taken to crematorium.
But when I heard the radio talking that the people survived in Sweden and this, I was always listening to it because even then, it was very hard to understand.
Even-- I saw all the horrors and all the misery and the destruction, but I thought, maybe somehow a young 39-year-old, maybe she survived.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This was the end of it.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
So I was home free with nobody, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I had to start a new life.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SNIFFS AND SIGHS] But you want to hear more.
How did you-- how did you end up in Israel?
Well, I-- they-- after six months or seven months, I became strong.
I came home.
I was 28 kilo, just like a-- good fat [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] little [NON-ENGLISH] or something, you know, very small.
[NON-ENGLISH] is goose or a duck.
A goose, yeah.
A goose Not a duck, a goose.
[INAUDIBLE] a goose. 28 kilo.
Very, very skinny.
And six or seven months later, I became a normal human being as much as I was able.
My hair looked like from an animal.
My hair was standing all over because when I got liberated, my head was all over with furuncles.
Furuncles, it's like-- it's a-- it's a-- it's a-- it's a sickness that it has-- it's very red, very painful, and it hurts terrible, all my head.
Also, during the camp, my hands were full with warts.
And as a young boy, I was thinking about it, how I'm going to come home with hands like this with warts all over.
They disappeared.
I don't know when and where, just disappeared.
So this was my occupation.
I was very occupied with it.
But after six or seven months, when I felt strong, they came-- shlichim from Israel.
And they tried to put us in the youth organizations and take us to all kind of camps.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went from one city to another, from one place to another.
And in 1947, in December, when the United Nations declared that Israel will be a state in the United Nations, we were dancing all over in Bratislava.
I decided that I want to be a soldier in Israel.
I was very young, but I said, I will be a soldier in Israel.
And in April, they came to--
April of 1948.
They came to take youth to-- to Israel.
And I volunteered, and we were taken to Prague.
We were about 18 or 20 young men and young women.
And we went into a small room from the Haganah.
And there was a table.
On the table was the Taanach, with the map of Israeli and a revolver.
And we went in one by one, and we had to swear on the Haganah of Israel.
But before we did this, the man said that if somebody wants-- if somebody doesn't feel right, he can leave, and he doesn't have to swear to be a soldier of the Israeli army.
You know, it was very primitive, like a partisan-like.
It was still illegal.
As a matter of fact, when we left Czechoslovakia, I left like a Hungarian.
My name was Chaim Bugarski.
And my papers were all there, like I was 19 years old.
When we-- when we crossed the train too from Czechoslovakia to Germany, the border patrol came and checked the papers.
And he saw that in my luggage was things wrapped in Czechoslovakian paper.
He says, how come that is-- you're Hungarian, and you have Czechoslovakian newspaper?
I said, because I spent a couple days in Czechoslovakia, so I took the newspaper.
Of course, he knew exactly who we are.
But this was the-- I want to ask you something that might not be clear for people in the future.
If the Jewish community-- if the Jews already knew that the State of Israel would be established, why were they pushing for immigration when it was still illegal?
What was the purpose to get all these people, young people, to Palestine if there was a hope that the State of Israel would be established?
Why were people risking their lives fighting the British?
This is something that may not be clear, especially to students.
Because this was something so important to us survivors of the camps that we didn't want to wait.
We wanted to have this tomorrow.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People that we lost everything, this was our-- this was the most important thing that I was able to think about it, that I be in my own country, and I'll be a soldier.
I'll be-- I'll be somebody strong.
It was very important.
To me, Palestine was already Israel.
To me, this was it.
I know that I don't have work to do.
I didn't like the Hungarians.
I didn't like the Germans.
I didn't like the Slovaks.
I hated the Hungarians even more than the Germans because of this-- of this incident on the bridge.
American-- Hungarian soldiers was yelling, dirty Jews, you're going to hell, so you don't need anything.
I hated the Hungarians and the Slovaks.
I mean, hated.
I just didn't like them because they-- how they behaved.

So I found out that all my uncles-- young, strong, good looking men-- died in the Hungarian munka tabor, in the Hungarian work camps, killed by Hungarians, not by Russians, killed because they were working hard-- You said not by Russians.
Did you mean Germans or Russians?
I mean, Germans or Russians.
They were fighting the Russians.
They were fighting on the Russian front.
But they were killed by the Hungarians.
So I didn't like them.
I didn't like them.
I know there is only one thing, Israel.
Definitely.
We didn't have nothing to do.
We came home after the war.
We had to stay many nights with revolvers, watching our homes, because they said that the [INAUDIBLE] are killing Jews.
We heard that in Poland, in Kielce, they killed Jews.
Yes!
Yes, yes!
We heard the [INAUDIBLE] were killing Jews that they came back.
They-- they don't want to give up.
The-- the war is over, and they are fighting.
In the hakhshara that we were in Bratislava, every night, we were standing.
We were watching.
Everybody was standing two or three hours with our revolver.
They taught us how to use revolvers, and we were watching our building because they know that the [INAUDIBLE] are killing Jews.
So we didn't like it.
We didn't like anymore our good neighbors.
Well, the ship was a pirate boat, wooden boat, that it was transferred to a diesel engine.
And 800 people on this little wooden boat.
We were taken to the Italian-French border and put on this-- in rubber dingies going to the boat, 800 of us, and took 11 days.
We came to Israel just a few days after the Declaration, 15th of May.
But the English were still in Israel.
So we landed in Caesarea.
The kibbutzniks came to the port, and we were thrown into the small fishing boats and taken to-- to a camp, to-- to some British army camp, abandoned British army camp near Hadera.
And after 11 days, we were given some food and to wash, to take showers.
And we became Israelis.
Unbelievable.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah.
It was beautiful.
